text: The robotic system provides magnified visualization and enhanced mobility, allowing the surgeon to perform a practical examination to confirm the distal margins, which are visibly well beyond the palpable rectal growth.

The surgeon mobilizes the rectal tube from the levator muscles, with the mesorectum coned and the vagina retracted anteriorly and superiorly. The right lateral and posterior dissections are performed, with magnification aiding in the identification of the rectal tube and levator muscles. The left lateral aspect of the rectal tube is also mobilized, revealing the cord, mesorectum, and bare rectal tube.
The surgeon uses a 60 mm stapler to secure the distal margins, applying it through the 12 mm port located in the right iliac fossa.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral approach, performing high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery. The mesocolon is fully mobilized along with the splenic flexure. The mesocolon is then divided just proximal to the divided inferior mesenteric pedicle, extending towards the marked proximal division of the colon.

A six-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision is made to extract the specimen, followed by the application of the anvil to the proximal rectal tube. The remaining rectal tube is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the vagina anteriorly and superiorly, revealing the dissected levator muscles surrounding the area. The mesocolon is visibly divided towards the marked proximal extent of the colon.
